coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation of environmental pressure effects on thermal runaway properties of 21700 lithium-ion batteries with high energy density

Liu, Changcheng
Huang, Que
Wang, Jian
coredata.dc:description:
As lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) with higher energy density are becoming more widely applied, especially in aviation field, understanding the potential thermal hazards of which at low pressure is beneficial to evaluate the safety during storage, transport and use. In this paper, thermal runaway (TR) behaviors of 21700 cylindrical LIBs with high energy density under various pressures were investigated, and characteristic parameters at low pressure such as surface temperature of LIBs and mass loss were analyzed. The results showed that combustion behaviors of LIBs could be weaker as the pressure decreased. Besides, T
mean was used to characterize the temperature variation of the TR. Several critical parameters such as T
open , T
tr , T max, and t
interval during the TR of LIBs were analyzed, respectively. The results indicated that T
tr followed an upward trend as the environmental pressure decreased from 101 to 30 kPa. The t
interval reduced from 48 s to 22 s when the pressure declined from 101 to 30 kPa. A heat transfer model under external heating could be used to explain the trend in TR influence at low pressure, and the temperature change rate (
d T
d t
) was related to the internal and external pressure of the battery. In addition, the relationship between the mass loss rate (MLR) and the pressure was analyzed, which indicated that environmental pressure had a little significance on mass loss for 21700 LIBs. Moreover, for given test conditions, the phenomenon of flame lift-off was not observed at different pressures, and the jet flame height reached the maximum at 50 kPa, which was 0.28 m. To quantify the maximum jet flame height, an approximate power law dependence between flame height and pressure could be deduced. This study might be helpful to further understand the fire risk of the LIB jet fires under low pressure.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
As lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) with higher energy density are becoming more widely applied, especially in aviation field, understanding the potential thermal hazards of which at low pressure is beneficial to evaluate the safety during storage, transport and use. In this paper, thermal runaway (TR) behaviors of 21700 cylindrical LIBs with high energy density under various pressures were investigated, and characteristic parameters at low pressure such as surface temperature of LIBs and mass loss were analyzed. The results showed that combustion behaviors of LIBs could be weaker as the pressure decreased. Besides, T
mean was used to characterize the temperature variation of the TR. Several critical parameters such as T
open , T
tr , T max, and t
interval during the TR of LIBs were analyzed, respectively. The results indicated that T
tr followed an upward trend as the environmental pressure decreased from 101 to 30 kPa. The t
interval reduced from 48 s to 22 s when the pressure declined from 101 to 30 kPa. A heat transfer model under external heating could be used to explain the trend in TR influence at low pressure, and the temperature change rate (
d T
d t
) was related to the internal and external pressure of the battery. In addition, the relationship between the mass loss rate (MLR) and the pressure was analyzed, which indicated that environmental pressure had a little significance on mass loss for 21700 LIBs. Moreover, for given test conditions, the phenomenon of flame lift-off was not observed at different pressures, and the jet flame height reached the maximum at 50 kPa, which was 0.28 m. To quantify the maximum jet flame height, an approximate power law dependence between flame height and pressure could be deduced. This study might be helpful to further understand the fire risk of the LIB jet fires under low pressure.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely used in various energy storage sites in recent years, because of their high energy density, environmental protection, and no memory effect, etc. [1–3]. In order to provide the driving range required for electric vehicles (EVs), the energy density of lithium-ion batteries is steadily increasing. Some producers have adopted the larger 21700 formats. The increased cell size results in greater capacity and energy density compared to the 18650 formats. However, active materials are used in LIBs in the pursuit of better electrochemical performance, which inevitable bring the potential fire risk and hazards [4–9]. Especially in the aviation field, the accident rate of LIBs is gradually increasing, which presents a problem to the safety aspects of LIBs [10–14]. Based on the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), there were more than three hundred aviation accidents involving battery fires from January 2006 to June 2021 [15,16]. Therefore, with the use of higher energy density LIBs, it is very important to study the characteristics and mechanisms of thermal runaway (TR) and prevention of fire accidents [9,17–19].

TR is a typical failure of battery that occurs when the heat release rate (HRR) of the internal chain reactions is greater than the external heat dissipation rate [16]. The TR behaviors of lithium batteries at atmospheric pressure under different modes of abuse has been extensively studied [3,20–24]. In recent years, the TR characteristics of LIBs at low pressure has been widely investigated [15,16,25–31]. For example, Chen et al. [30,31] studied the TR behavior of LIBs at low pressure using a field experiment approach, and ambient pressure was found to have a remarkable effect on the ignition time, mass loss and HRR. Fu et al. [25] studied the ignition and combustion characteristics of batteries under an external incident heat flux at low pressure and showed that low pressure prolongs the ignition time (in a relatively big range of 30–101 kPa atmospheric pressures) and attenuates the combustion intensity of batteries. And an empirical model is established to predict the mean mass loss rate (MLR) of LIBs under low pressure. Liu et al. [16] showed that the safety venting time will advance and the internal reaction intensity of the battery will decrease as pressure drops. Sun et al. [28] indicated that the area distribution model can be employed to assess and forecast the TR risks in different SOCs and pressure situations. Chen et al. [32] studied the effects of ambient pressure and heating power on the TR characteristics in a dynamic pressure chamber and showed that the effects of ambient pressure on the TR fire behaviors mainly attribute to the low oxygen density. Xie et al. [33] showed that the reduction of pressure leads to the delay of voltage drop time, the reduction of TR intensity and temperature, and the U-shaped change of TR time. Liu et al. [34] studied the effects of different arrangements at low pressure on the TR characteristics of LIBs and showed that the intensity of chemical reaction is more violent under 96 kPa which cause a lower onset temperature and a shorter onset time than that under 61 kPa. Wang et al. [35] concluded that with the decrease of ambient pressure, the TR trigger time becomes longer and the maximum surface temperature decrease. Jia et al. [15] found that the characteristics of TR propagation with of TR propagation are different and the TR behaviors become weaker and the average maximum temperature of modules decreases 20–50 °C as the pressure decreases.

However, although much significant work has been done on TR, the previous research findings are fairly mixed in low pressure environments. For instance, Fu et al. [25] indicated that the 40 kPa will delay the TR in single cells. However, Chen et al., Liu et al. and Xie et al. observed a more rapid TR for LIBs under low pressure [16,26,31]. Therefore, the pressure effect on the TR process can also be more complex. Furthermore, in contrast to the widely studied 18650 LIBs, although a number of studies have been carried out to examine the TR behavior of 21700 LIBs with higher energy density, there is still a lack of careful analytical work on the fire characteristics of batteries during TR, in particular at different air pressures [36–39].

In this work, a low chamber that can simulate different altitudes was utilized to deeply investigate the TR behavior of 21700 LIBs with high energy density at low pressure environment ranging from 30 kPa to 101 kPa. Specific experimental information, including the TR process, the surface temperature of LIBs, mass loss and flame height are provided and a heat transfer model in TR of LIBs under external heating is established. In addition, the flame height of jet fire is further analyzed based on the turbulent jet diffusion flame theory, which is crucial for the fire risk of LIBs under low pressure. The research results will be further helpful to insight the effects of the environmental pressure and offer a guidance for the storage and transport of LIBs [40–42].

Commercial 21700 LIBs of lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) cathode (Tesla Model 3 EVs, 21 mm in diameter and 70 mm in height) were used in this work [38]. This battery has a rated capacity and voltage of 5 Ah and 3.7 V, with a lower and upper voltage limit of 2.75 and 4.2 V, respectively. Before the tests, batteries were cycled twice between 2.75 and 4.2 V and activated with a Constant Current-Constant Voltage (CCCV) protocol at 0.5 C using a cell testing system (NEWARE CT-4008,5V/6A) [16]. Subsequently, the batteries were charged to 100% SOC using the CCCV mode. After this, all batteries were rested for 24 h to avoid the effects of internal heating between charging and discharging [43].
A chamber of an internal volume 12 m3 was designed to provide a well-controlled environment (pressures vary from 15 to 101 kPa) in Fig. 1 .

Inside the chamber, a heating furnace of 2 kW was applied as the external heating source to initiate the TR and was placed on a raised platform covered by a fire-proof board. The heat flux of different heights (H 15cm, H 20cm, H 30cm, H 35cm, H 40cm) above the center of the heating furnace was provided by Fig. 1.

As shown in Figs. 1 and 3 K-type thermocouples (T
1 , T
2 , T
3 ) was attached on the center surface of the battery, and other 7 thermocouples (T
4 , T
5 , T
6 , T
7 , T
8 , T
9 , T
10 ) were placed vertically above the battery to detect the flame temperature. The data acquisition equipment (NI cDAQ-9174) can be used to record the temperature. An electric balance was used to measure the mass loss of batteries. The two radiometer sensors (TS-34C) with 4.01 μV/(W/m2) were located 35 cm in the horizontal and 40 cm in the vertical and 35 cm horizontally and 50 cm vertically, respectively. In the experiments, two cameras (50 fps and 100 fps) and an infrared imager (Forward Looking Infra-Red, FLIR, T650sc2.1) were applied to record the flame images. In this work, 101, 90, 70, 50, and 30 kPa were considered in Table 1 , to illustrate the effects of the low pressure in the TR characteristics and the flame height of jet fire during over-heating.

The following is a detailed description of the experimental process using 70 kPa as an example. After the measuring instrument has been set up properly, the low-pressure chamber hatch is first closed, as shown in Fig. 1. Start the low-pressure chamber pump, so that the pressure inside the chamber reaches the 70 kPa. Switch on the heater from the outside according to the wiring holes reserved for the low-pressure compartment. At the same time, the TR processes, flame images, and the temperature mass loss were recorded by the cameras, the FLIR, the thermocouples and the electric balance. The ejected objects, including the copper foil, the carbon-based anode material, were observed in Fig. 1. To reduce experimental error, each set of experiments was repeated twice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat transfer mechanism of LIBs under external heating
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 is a simplified diagram of heat transfer and gas release and jet fire model of cells. The temperature of the cell will rise when the cell is exposed to the external sources. Subsequently, this is followed by many exothermic reactions, including the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), positive and negative decomposition, and their couple reactions in the batteries, which produce gases (H2, CO2, CO, CH4, etc.) [15,31,38].

For the LIBs, the main factor for the safety valve opening is the evaporation of the electrolyte [2,44]. As shown in Fig. 2 , in this study, in order to characterize the thermal transfer effect on the TR of LIBs, the energy required to empty the LIBs in the experiment and the energy provided by the external heating sources can be given by Eq. (1) and Eq. (2) [15].

Therefore, according to Eq. (13), the temperature change rate (
d T
d t
) is related to the internal and external pressure of the battery. Therefore, the rate of temperature rise during TR of LIBs is not only determined by the complex internal exothermic reaction but also related to the external environmental pressure.

Fig. 3 shows the complete TR process of LIBs of 100% SOC, from ignition to extinguishment. As shown in Fig. 3 , the LIBs combustion behaviors can be divided into the following several stages: (1) heating, and venting behavior; (2) sparks; (3) jet fire; (4) from stable combustion to extinguishment. The initial phases of ignition are masked by the emission of white smoke and gases, is known as the venting behavior, and the FLIR images of the venting under five pressures were shown in Fig. 3.

With the decrease in ambient pressure, venting flow gave the similar trends. More emissions of gas and smoke were observed, and a plume rose vertically, propelled by the buoyancy [38]. Then, a number of smoke was being released for a short period of time, showing that the TR was occurring [45]. On functioning of the cell pressure relief mechanism, white smoke and gases were emitted when the battery relief regime was in action, which was thought to consist mainly of combustible gases (CO2, CO, CH4, H2, etc.) and electrolyte vapors [15,16,30,46]. Venting carried on as such until the initial eruption of sparks was noted, becoming brighter and brighter. The combustible materials ejected with air mixtures were then ignited by the high temperature and created a successive jet combustion process until all of combustible gases were exhausted and the fire suppressed [38,47]. There is a believe that the jet sparks may have functioned as ignition sources for vented gases. At the end of the experiments, the carbon-based anode materials were seen to eject from the cells in the midst of the Stage (2) and the Stage (3) falling to the ground [48]. Some of the previous investigation may offer a perceptive insight and back up the above findings [38].

In the course of TR, the heat component of the cells is continually building up and the temperature rapidly becomes higher. As a result, a great deal of decomposing combustible substances and gases are produced in the cells [25]. These chemical reactions will lead to the very high internal temperature of the cell and a gradual growth. As an excessive internal pressure accumulates, it can surpass the structural stress and external pressure of the pressure relief valve, resulting in cracking of the valve. Then, a massive quantity of gas and combustible materials are ejected from the cells, which causing a fire and explosion, as shown in venting process of Fig. 3. Fig. 2 illustrates a simplification of the pressure at the relief valve during rupture, where this pressure is governed by the competitive internal pressure and ambient pressures. For comparison with normal pressure 101 kPa, the partial pressure difference is specified as the different cracked pressure of the relief valve (

Differences in relief valve cracking pressure have an effect on the combustion behavior of LIBs. As seen by the difference in relief valve rupture pressure, 11 kPa, 31 kPa, 51 kPa, and 71 kPa is required for LIBs to crack. One may conclude that the safety valve of LIBs will open more and more rapidly under low ambient pressure. Therefore, there is a need to focus more on TR and cracking with LIBs at the low pressure. Particularly, in aircraft and spaceships, where LIBs with high energy density perform at low-pressure and the threshold pressure of the safety valve of LIBs may be adjusted to a lower-than-normal value for the better safety characteristics [15,16,25,31].

Fig. 4 illustrates the surface temperature profiles with three thermocouples at different cells positions for 21700 LIB TR of 100% SOC under 90 kPa during external heating abuse process. In general, as the battery TR evolves, several phenomena are typical, for example, safety valve cracks, gas emission, sparks, TR, and so on [38].

As depicted on Fig. 4 , the TR process at the 90 kPa may be separated into three steps: (1) heating; (2) venting; (3) TR. In stage (1), it can be seen that the T
1 , T
2 , and T
3 grew linearly due to the continuous heating of the external source. Subsequently, because of the onset of exothermic reactions and the large accumulation of intracellular gas production, the safety valve broke accompanied by a slight cooling (Stage (2)). However, once the safety valve opened, a substantial quantity of smoke was given off for a short space of time, including more flammable gases, such as H2, CO, CH4 and so on [46,49]. Finally, the cell surface temperature showed a significant elevation. Following this, the cell surface temperature climbed drastically until the occurrence of TR.

As shown in Fig. 4, the temperature variation trends of T
1 , T
2 , and T
3 were basically the same, and especially the average temperature variation rule of T
1 , T
2 , and T
3 (T
mean ) almost coincided with the T
3 . Considered that T
1 and T
3 were located on the surface 1 cm from the negative and positive electrodes of the battery, respectively and T
2 was located at the center surface of the cells, according to past experimental studies [50,51], the axial temperature difference of LIBs decreases during vertical heating. However, in current work, the surface temperature of the LIBs in the vertical direction from the bottom to the top were T
1 , T
3 and T
2 . According to the research results of the TR front by Zhang et al., that is probably that the TR is triggered by different methods, there is intense heat release inside the battery near the cathode and anode poles and the temperature gradient is correlated with the thermophysical properties [52]. However, the specific causes need to be further studied. Therefore, to characterize the surface temperature of the LIBs at the external heat source, T
mean is defined as the mean surface temperature of the cells which can be adopted to illustrate the temperature variation of the TR in Fig. 4. And, because the heating furnace was not turned on during the test phase, the time of t
open , t
tr , and t max should be subtracted from this period. Nevertheless, unlike the unfilled charged batteries, the TR behavior of batteries with 100% SOC triggered a dramatic combustion, in accordance with the previously recorded observation [19,53–56]. Therefore, it is necessary to guard against the TR of LIBs in fully charged cells.

The effect of pressures on TR process is further explored through Eq. (13), Fig. 5 shows the surface temperature variation profile of 21700 LIBs for 100% SOC under low pressure, including 101, 90, 70, 50, and 30 kPa (the temperature change curve of T
mean under different environmental pressures).

It is clear that the temperature evolution curves show a similar trend for TR behaviors at the five pressures. Therefore, according to Figs. 4 and 5, due to two critical moments of safety venting and TR which are divided into three phrases, four crucial factors for safety venting temperature T
open , TR onset temperature T
tr , maximum surface temperature after TR T max, and the time interval from the safety venting to TR t
interval (T
open , T
tr , T max, and t
interval are respectively the mean value of two repeated experiments at the same pressure in Table 2 ) are used to investigate the pressure effect on the TR (in Fig. 6 ). And Table 2 concludes the key parameters of two repeated experiments at the same pressure under different ambient pressures.

As mentioned earlier, a cracking of the safety valve is a bridging moment from heating to ventilation in the TR process. Former investigation has found that the safety exhaust temperature T
open is tightly coupled with the positive materials and SOC level of batteries [57,58]. As shown in Fig. 6, for 100% SOC batteries at 101 kPa, 90 kPa, 70 kPa, 50 kPa and 30 kPa, T
open are respectively 191 °C, 200 °C, 216 °C, 197 °C, and 206 °C, which is nor sensitive to the environmental pressure. The TR onset temperature (T
tr ) and the maximum surface temperature (T max) after TR are two essential factors which are helpful to assess the thermal stability and thermal risks of LIBs. As seen in the green bar graph in Fig. 6, T
tr for 100% SOC batteries at 101, 90, 70, 50 and 30 kPa are 230 °C, 234 °C, 237 °C, 239 °C, and 232 °C, and T max are 635 °C, 768 °C, 666 °C, 745 °C, 703 °C, respectively. As a result, with the T
tr follows an upward trend as the environmental pressure decreases. However, T max performs also an increasing trend, which is inconsistent with the study by Liu et al. [16] and may be due to the heating method, larger low pressure chamber, higher energy density batteries or other reasons of uncertainty. Additionally, Fig. 6 compares the t
interval under the five pressure levels, where t
interval decreases from 48 s to 22 s varying from 101 kPa to 30 kPa, however, Liu et al. [16] found that t
interval increases under several cells when the air pressure drops from 100 kPa to 20 kPa. In summary, it indicates that differences from those characteristic temperatures may be affected by ambient pressure, ambient temperature, heating method, the cell number, the battery types and so on. Whereas the overall effect of pressure is complicated, where it is hard to simply conclude that lowering the environmental pressure may increase (reduce) the thermal safety of the cells [15,16,25,49].

Fig. 7 investigates the mass loss of 21700 LIBs with high energy density at different ambient pressures. In the initial 800 s, there was almost mass loss for LIBs in the experiments. And as the safety valve cracks, the mass decreases slightly and a number of white smoke ejected from the cells [2]. Then, there is a dramatic increase in mass, with most of the mass loss occurring in this phrase. Finally, the mass loss approaches a constant, as most of the combustible gas has been consumed [59]. The mass loss peak is generated by the counter-acting force at the start of the ejecting.

The measured mass loss data are given in Table 3 and illustrate the great difference at different pressures. As can be seen in Table 3, the rebound force at atmospheric pressure is the highest, at 519 g. Thus, a high SOC LIBs has a higher amount of instability and combustible materials than a lower SOC cell, and it will eject violently for 100% SOC 21700 LIBs [38].

In general, for fire researches, the pressure effect on the burning is extremely sophisticated. The changes in ambient pressure can alter the oxygen mass concentration and combustion reaction rate, resulting in different combustion rates. However, differently from fires with ordinary hydrocarbon solid and liquid fuels, in which the combustion rate is controlled by radiation and convective heat feedback through the flame to fuel surface, the rate of combustion in LIBs is mainly determined with a trade off in the rate of heat release from the TR reaction inside the cell and the loss of heat to the environment area [60–62]. However, according to the study of low pressure by Chen et al. [30,31], the MLR (mass loss rate) and the pressure can be expressed as:
(15)
m ′
= 0.0025 n
P 0.8775
where n is the cell number. Based on Eq. (15), the pressure effect should be available for the LIBs fire. However, in this experiment, in terms of mass loss, mass loss of 101 kPa, 90 kPa, 70 kPa, 50 kPa and 30 kPa were 36.27 g, 39.93 g, 38.36 g, 41.70 g and 33.16 g respectively. It indicated that environmental pressure has a little significance on mass loss for 21700 LIBs which can be the combined impact of the higher energy density batteries and low pressure [15,16,25].

The sparks were the first visible evidence of combustion, occurring before the battery had fully caught fire as shown in Fig. 3. Subsequently, in the early stages of jet fire development were observed in Fig. 3. In conclusion, jet sparks were predominant in the early stage before the mix of the jet sparks and flame from the batteries, and eventually the jet flame become dominant [63,64].

Although many studies have been conducted to explore the flame geometrical and thermal properties of jet fires under different ambient conditions, the available studies on jet fires related to the TR of LIBs is still lacking, especially at low pressure conditions [2,65–67]. For the turbulent jet diffusion flame, the fuel is ejected from the orifice of the safety valve and the oxygen is entrained into the flame to react with the fuel. Considering the relatively short duration of the battery jet flame, unlike conventional gas jet fires [68–71], in this work, the frame-by-frame method was used to calculate the maximum jet flame height of LIBs. However, the phenomenon of flame lift-off was not observed during this study even if the ambient pressure was reduced which was different from the large-format LFP cells with 100% SOC in previous study [72,73]. Fig. 8 shows the tendency of maximum jet flame height with the ambient pressure.

As shown in Fig. 8, it is observed that the maximum jet flame height presented an increasing trend in the reduced pressure atmosphere, which was basically consistent with previous research [69]. The maximum jet flame height was 0.22 m, 0.28 m, 0.21 m, 0.20 m, 0.18 m in the combustion chamber at 30, 50, 70, 90 and 101 kPa, respectively. However, it can be seen that the maximum jet flame height reached the maximum at 50 kPa, which was 0.28 m in Fig. 8. From the fitting curve results of Fig. 8, an approximate power law dependence between flame height and pressure can be deduced. In previous investigations, Most et al. [74] indicated that the flame height increased with pressure for pressures below 80 kPa and decreased with pressure for pressures above that level (it means that the 80 kPa may be a critical value). Whereas, as shown in Fig. 8, this may be indicated that a critical pressure for the maximum jet flame height of LIB fires will be occurred with the decrease of ambient pressure. The exact reasons for this will further require a lot of subsequent experiments, including different types of batteries, to investigate in depth. In the experiments, because of the brief jet fire and the poor repeatability of jet flames, in the future the jet fire from large format LIBs will be studied. At the same time, according to the gas-solid mixture produced by the TR of LIBs, it will be possible to investigate gas-solid jet fires by mixing the gas-solid combustibles produced by the LIBs referring to the method of studying the conventional jet fires [40,66,75,76]. It is helpful to further understand the fire risk of LIBs jet fire especially under low pressure [44,77–92].

This work comprehensively analyzed the TR characteristics of 21700 LIBs with the high specific energy in low pressure from 30 kPa to 101 kPa. Furthermore, the flame height at low pressure is also innovatively carried out. The main conclusion of this study are as follows:
(1)
The release of a large amount of smoke in a short period of time indicates the occurrence of TR. During the TR process, the heat levels of the cells accumulate and the temperature rises rapidly. With regard to the operation of the battery venting mechanism, it may be derived that LIBs will burst more easily and more quickly under sub-pressure.
(2)
After the safety valve opens, the cell surface temperature climbs drastically until the occurrence of TR. T
tr shows an increasing trend with the pressure cooling. Unlike previous studies, in this study, t
interval decreases from 48 s to 22 s when the environmental pressure drops from 101 kPa to 30 kPa.
(3)
Unlike the mass loss of ordinary hydrocarbon solid and liquid fuels, the results indicate that environmental pressure has a little significance on mass loss for 21700 LIBs which can be the combined impact of the higher energy density batteries and low pressure.
(4)
The phenomenon of flame lift-off is not observed during this study at different pressures. Also, it indicated that the maximum jet flame height reached the maximum at 50 kPa, which was 0.28 m. To quantify the maximum jet flame height, an approximate power law dependence between flame height and pressure can be deduced. This may be indicated that a critical pressure for the maximum jet flame height of LIB fires will be occurred with the decrease of environmental pressure.
